text: So I do a bunch of videos that are fun or interesting. This video is a little different in the sense that I think this is a really important video.

One issue that comes up all the time as I go out into the community and proctor new robotic surgeons or surgeons come and visit us at our mentorship site is what if I get into bleeding while I'm doing a robotic lung resection?

You know, oh my God, I feel like I'm so far away from that patient asleep. on the operating room table. And the reality is if you do enough of these robotic lung resections, you are going to get into bleeding once in a while. It shouldn't happen often. I quote patients about one percent of the time. I do about a hundred cases a year. So it happens about once a year.
And I'm going to show some actual video from my last two conversions for bleeding, which were fourteen months apart. And both patients did fine. Neither one required a blood transfusion.
But There's a sequence of steps that I think you should be familiar with. I think your team should be familiar with.

And you should probably do fire drills like we do at our hospital intermittently so everybody in the team understands here's what happens if we get into bleeding and we have to convert to an open case. It should be done carefully, safely,

During a left-sided lobectomy, the surgeon encounters bleeding and uses the Cartier Arm One to apply pressure with a sponge on the pulmonary artery or vein. The camera is repositioned through the assistant port to maintain a clear view of the bleeding site.

The surgeon removes the lateral instruments in preparation for converting to a posterolateral thoracotomy. The medial arm continues to apply pressure on the bleeder, which is visible through the camera.

Resist the temptation to try and connect the dots. That's going to be too low. What you want is you want to do your standard posterior lateral thoracotomy. And once you get in, you get manual control of the bleeding. And at that point, because this arm is just holding pressure, you can just pull it out. And then... you can move the whole robot out. So it should always be slow. It should always be controlled.

I'm trying to think if there was anything else I needed to tell them. Oh, so let's say you did grab. With your medial arm, you grab the bleeder. Instead of holding pressure, you actually have it grab. Well, the thing you want to remember is you can't just slide the instrument out because you'll tear the bleeder. So there's an emergency release, emergency instrument release key. I think Chanel holds it up there. It has to be on your cart. Everybody who does robotic thoracic surgery should have that key. Or you can have a second surgeon go to the console and release it. But either way, you know, the key will release it. But I prefer just to apply pressure with the medial arm. And that way you can just slide it off. And we do this fire drill every once in a while here. So everybody in the room knows exactly what to do. And the conversions always go really smoothly.
The surgeon performs a left lower lobectomy, focusing on a challenging dissection around a positive hilar node. The node was previously sampled with EBUS, but the results were false negative.

The surgeon places two spondyloles in the chest, with one positioned above the dissection site. As bleeding occurs, the surgeon uses a spondylole to apply pressure to the pulmonary artery, a low-pressure system. The camera is briefly obscured by blood, but is promptly cleaned to maintain visibility.

The surgeon uses a suction device to remove blood from the surgical field while maintaining pressure on the bleeder in the pulmonary artery. The arms remain in place to control the situation, avoiding the temptation to use a Cartier instrument with the tip up, which could worsen the tear. The surgeon steps away from the console to ensure pressure is held for three minutes, aiming to stop the bleeding and communicate reassurance to the surgical team.

We're not bleeding to death here. I talk to the anesthesiologist. I get blood in the room. We may have to convert. I talk to the team, open the thoracotomy tray. We may have to convert. All this is happening. And three minutes go by and then I want to look again because I want to see if the bleeding is controlled. Sometimes just pressure can control it and you can finish the case. In fact, I'd say probably about half the time that's the situation. So here I'm just going to take a look. It looks a little better but you'll see that it's one of those situations where I don't feel like we're going to be able to continue the case. Now the issue is, can you actually throw a stitch robotically?

Some people do this. I think that's a little aggressive. I think you're skating on thin ice if you do that. If I have to throw a stitch to control the bleeder, unless it's a very distal branch and it's something very minor, I'm going to convert to a thoracotomy. I do not convert to vats, not for bleeding. I convert to a posterolateral thoracotomy.

The surgeon holds pressure using the medial robotic arm (arm one) during a left-sided resection to control bleeding. The lateral three robotic arms, including the camera port in arm two, are removed in preparation for a thoracotomy.

The surgeon holds pressure on the bleeder using the medial robotic arm while preparing for a posterolateral thoracotomy. The camera is repositioned from arm two to the assistant port, maintaining visualization of the sponge stick on the bleeder to ensure bleeding control. The lateral three robotic ports are removed to facilitate the thoracotomy.

After completing the posterolateral thoracotomy, the surgeon manually controls the bleeding through the thoracotomy incision. Arm one of the robotic system is removed, allowing the entire robot to be safely moved out of the surgical field.

The surgeon attempts an anteromedial basilar segmentectomy on the left lower lobe, working on small pulmonary artery branches in a confined space. The robotic stapler is fired across the area, leading to bleeding. A sponge roll is visible in the chest to hold pressure, and the surgeon uses a Cartier instrument to manage the situation. The assistant uses suction to control the bleeding, while preparations for thoracotomy conversion are underway due to the bleeding.

The surgeon maintains pressure on the bleeder using the medial robotic arm, while the assistant removes the camera from arm two and places it through the assistant port for continuous visualization. The lateral robotic ports are removed, and the surgeon steps away from the console to scrub for a posterolateral thoracotomy through the fifth intercostal space. The robotic arm holding pressure is slid out, and the robot is moved aside to gain manual control of the bleeder.

And I'll control the bleeder with a stitch or whatever needs to be done through the thoracotomy. And these are the steps of a safe conversion. So the main takeaway point is that conversion to thoracotomy for bleeding during robotic lung resection can be done safely, should be done safely. It's just a sequence of steps. You have to have a plan like anything else in surgery. You need to be prepared. The appropriate equipment is in the room. The team should be familiar with these steps. You should practice them with fire drills as necessary. But having been through a few of these now, I can tell you that I believe in my heart that robotic surgery, is the best way to do major lung resection, and it is the safest way to do major lung resection. The patient is never in any danger as long as you have a plan and you're prepared. Again, I appreciate you watching these videos. I look forward to your comments. You can always get a hold of me. My contact information is available through my website, roboticthoracic.surgery, and I wish you good luck, and I look forward to hearing from you. Thanks.
